judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently and correctly identify the same root cause, fix, and wipe/rebuild explanation with equivalent depth and file-level specificity; they are comparable in quality.

report_1.evidence: Postgres row-level security does not apply to either of those cases: a superuser bypasses RLS entirely, and a table owner is exempt from RLS by default unless the table is declared FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. db/migrations/0001_init.sql only does ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY (lines 21 and 25), never FORCE.

report_2.evidence: in PostgreSQL, ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY does not apply policies to the table's owner — the owner (and superusers / BYPASSRLS roles) skip RLS silently unless the table also has FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, which this migration never sets. So even though deps.tenant_connection correctly runs SET app.current_tenant, the policy is simply never evaluated for the cedar_owner connection. Every request sees every tenant's rows, with no error or warning.

report_1.evidence: Point the application at the dedicated, unprivileged role: change DATABASE_URL in .env/.env.example to postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar... Going forward, the application should always connect as cedar_app (owner/superuser roles reserved for migrations and admin only)... The core one-line fix is the role in DATABASE_URL; the migration hardening is the safety net.

report_2.evidence: Primary: point the application's DATABASE_URL at the least-privilege cedar_app role instead of cedar_owner (update .env/.env.example to postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar)... Going forward, adopt the strict two-role model: cedar_owner (schema owner) is used only to run migrations; the running app connects only as the least-privilege cedar_app.

report_1.evidence: cedar_app is created only by db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql, which — per its own header comment, docs/local-setup.md, and README.md — is a one-time manual step, explicitly not part of database initialization or migrations... The -v deletes the cedar_pgdata volume..., destroying the entire Postgres cluster — and roles are cluster-global objects, so cedar_app is destroyed with it. up -d db then creates a fresh cluster whose only role is the bootstrap cedar_owner, and the reset script proceeds straight to migrate.sh, which recreates tables but not the role... To make it work again, the cedar_app role must be re-created in the fresh cluster: re-run psql ... -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql.

report_2.evidence: The cedar_app role lives only in db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql, which is explicitly not part of automatic initialization (its header comment says so) and is only a manual one-time step in docs/local-setup.md. Crucially, reset-local-db.sh re-runs migrate.sh but does not re-run the role bootstrap. So after a reset, cedar_app no longer exists... To make it work again, the create_app_role.sql bootstrap must be re-run against the fresh cluster, e.g. psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql
